% Render a figure if its PDF exists yet, else a labelled placeholder box. Lets the
% pre-registered draft compile before the run produces figures.
\newcommand{\figorbox}[2]{%
  \IfFileExists{../figures/#1}{\includegraphics[width=#2\linewidth]{#1}}{%
    \fbox{\parbox[c][0.30\linewidth][c]{#2\linewidth}{\centering\itshape
    [figure generated after the experimental run]}}}}

\title{The Validation Gap: When and Why Offline Metrics Fail to Predict Robot Policy Success}
\author{Eswar Krishnan}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Imitation-learned manipulation policies are deployed by choosing a single checkpoint from the
many saved during training. Because measuring task success requires expensive robot rollouts,
practitioners select this checkpoint using validation loss on held-out expert actions, a
criterion inherited from supervised learning. This proxy is known to be unreliable: the
checkpoint with the lowest validation loss can be substantially worse than the best one
achievable, yet the magnitude of this discrepancy, its causes, and its dependence on task
properties remain uncharacterized, and no offline alternative has been evaluated
systematically. We name this discrepancy the \emph{validation gap} and study it as a
surrogate-endpoint calibration problem, analogous to validating a clinical biomarker against
the outcome it is meant to predict. We present a pre-registered and reproducible framework
that trains Diffusion Policy and ACT across eight LIBERO and Robomimic tasks spanning
short-to-long horizons and light-to-heavy contact, saves dense checkpoints along each training
run, and scores every checkpoint with eight offline metrics computed without environment
interaction together with ground-truth closed-loop success. The framework tests whether the
gap exists, whether it depends on task regime, whether any offline metric predicts success
more faithfully than validation loss, and whether a composite predictor generalizes to
held-out tasks. It further examines whether metrics of global trajectory coherence, such as
distributional drift from the training manifold and open-loop replay fidelity, predict success
better than metrics of local action accuracy. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first
systematic calibration of offline policy-selection metrics against deployment success in
robotic manipulation. The framework requires no robot hardware and runs on consumer GPUs.
\end{abstract}
